questions.text: Cheetahs are the fastest land animals in the world.
questions.explanation: They can reach speeds up to 60-70 mph.

questions.text: A group of crows is called a 'murder'.
questions.explanation: Yes, it is true! It comes from old folk tales.

questions.text: Bats are completely blind.
questions.explanation: Bats can actually see quite well, some even better than humans in low light.

questions.text: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins.
questions.explanation: By slowing their heart rate, sloths can hold their breath for up to 40 minutes!

questions.text: Bullfrogs do not sleep.
questions.explanation: They enter periods of resting but do not experience deep sleep.

questions.text: Koalas fingerprints are so close to humans that they can taint crime scenes.
questions.explanation: Even under a microscope, their fingerprints are nearly indistinguishable from humans.

questions.text: Goldfish have a three-second memory.
questions.explanation: Goldfish actually have a memory span of several months.

questions.text: Wombat poop is cube-shaped.
questions.explanation: This shape stops the poop from rolling away and marks their territory.

questions.text: An octopus has four hearts.
questions.explanation: An octopus actually has exactly three hearts.

questions.text: The Turritopsis dohrnii jellyfish is biologically immortal.
questions.explanation: It can revert completely to a sexually immature stage after having reached sexual maturity.

questions.text: Pigs cannot look up into the sky.
questions.explanation: The anatomy of their neck prevents them from looking straight up.

questions.text: Ostriches bury their heads in the sand when scared.
questions.explanation: They lie low to hide or turn eggs in their nests, but do not bury their heads.

questions.text: A snail can sleep for three years.
questions.explanation: They can hibernate for years if the weather is not suitable for survival.

questions.text: Dolphins can only sleep fully submerged underwater.
questions.explanation: Dolphins sleep at the surface with one eye open, keeping half their brain awake.

questions.text: Bulls are enraged by the color red.
questions.explanation: Bulls are colorblind; the movement of the cape angers them, not the color.

questions.text: Elephants can jump about 6 inches off the ground.
questions.explanation: Elephants cannot jump at all due to their bone structure and weight.

questions.text: A cockroach can live for a week without its head.
questions.explanation: They breathe through body spiracles and die only from thirst.

questions.text: Polar bears have white skin beneath their fur.
questions.explanation: Their skin is black to absorb sunlight; only the fur appears white.

questions.text: Owls can rotate their heads a full 360 degrees.
questions.explanation: They can only turn their heads 270 degrees, not a full circle.

questions.text: A flea can jump 350 times its own body length.
questions.explanation: That is equivalent to a human jumping the length of a football field.

questions.text: Honeybees have four eyes.
questions.explanation: Honeybees actually have five eyes — two compound and three simple ocelli.

questions.text: Most penguins mate for life.
questions.explanation: Many species only stay together for one breeding season.

questions.text: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.
questions.explanation: Their unique shoulder joint allows for full rotational flight.

questions.text: Crocodiles shed tears when eating.
questions.explanation: Swallowing pumps air into sinuses near their eyes, causing a frothing effect.

questions.text: Mice love cheese above all other foods.
questions.explanation: Mice actually prefer sweet and grain-based foods over cheese.

questions.text: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance.
questions.explanation: Perhaps the most fitting collective noun in the animal kingdom.

questions.text: Starfish have no brain.
questions.explanation: They have a decentralized nervous system spread across their entire body.

questions.text: Sea otters drift apart during sleep because they lack social bonds.
questions.explanation: Sea otters actually hold hands while sleeping to stay together as a raft.

questions.text: A shrimp heart is located in its head.
questions.explanation: All of a shrimp vital organs are packed into the cephalothorax.

questions.text: Flamingos are born pink because of genetics.
questions.explanation: Flamingos are actually born white; their pink color comes from carotenoids in their diet.

questions.text: A group of owls is called a parliament.
questions.explanation: Owls have long been associated with wisdom and governance.

questions.text: Sharks must keep swimming or they will die.
questions.explanation: Most sharks can rest on the ocean floor; only some obligate ram ventilators must keep moving.

questions.text: Butterflies taste with their feet.
questions.explanation: They have chemoreceptors on their legs to identify plants for egg-laying.

questions.text: A group of cats is called a clowder.
questions.explanation: Lesser known collective nouns are common in English for animal groups.

questions.text: Wombats produce square-shaped droppings.
questions.explanation: Their intestines are shaped to produce cube-like scat to mark territory.

questions.text: Lobsters are immortal.
questions.explanation: They do not weaken with age but still die from disease, predation, or molting.

questions.text: Cows are solitary creatures that prefer being alone.
questions.explanation: Research shows cows form close bonds with best friends and become stressed when separated.

questions.text: A blue whale heart is so large a child could swim through its arteries.
questions.explanation: The heart weighs around 400 pounds and is the size of a small car.

questions.text: Snakes can hear through their jawbone.
questions.explanation: They detect vibrations through ground contact with their jaw.

questions.text: Honeybees die after stinging because their stinger stays in mammals.
questions.explanation: The barbed stinger gets lodged and tears away part of the bee abdomen.

questions.text: A group of jellyfish is called a bloom or a smack.
questions.explanation: Both terms are accepted collective nouns for jellyfish.

questions.text: Gorillas cannot swim.
questions.explanation: They lack the instinctive paddle reflex and generally avoid deep water.

questions.text: Horses can sleep standing up but can only dream lying down.
questions.explanation: They need REM sleep which requires lying down.

questions.text: Octopuses have one heart, like most sea creatures.
questions.explanation: Octopuses actually have three hearts and copper-based blue blood.

questions.text: Pigeons have been awarded military medals.
questions.explanation: Carrier pigeons like Cher Ami were decorated for service in World War I.

questions.text: Cats always land on their feet.
questions.explanation: They have a righting reflex but still sustain injuries from falls, especially short ones.

questions.text: Reindeer eyes change color between summer and winter.
questions.explanation: Their eyes shift from gold in summer to blue in winter to improve vision in low arctic light.

questions.text: A mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet.
questions.explanation: It strikes at 50 mph and can shatter aquarium glass.

questions.text: Clownfish can change sex.
questions.explanation: When the dominant female dies, the dominant male transitions to female.

questions.text: Dogs sweat through their paws.
questions.explanation: Merocrine sweat glands are located in their paw pads.

questions.text: The sun rises in the East and sets in the West.
questions.explanation: Due to Earth's rotation, this is true.

questions.text: Water makes up about 71% of the Earth's surface.
questions.explanation: Oceans hold about 96.5% of all Earth's water.

questions.text: Lightning never strikes the same place twice.
questions.explanation: Lightning constantly strikes tall structures like the Empire State Building.

questions.text: Venus is the hottest planet in our solar system.
questions.explanation: Even though Mercury is closer, Venus has a thick atmosphere that traps heat.

questions.text: Sound travels faster in air than in water.
questions.explanation: Sound actually travels much faster in water because particles are closer together.

questions.text: Bananas are strictly considered berries, but strawberries are not.
questions.explanation: Botanically, bananas are berries, while strawberries are 'aggregate fruits'.

questions.text: One million Earths could fit inside the Sun.
questions.explanation: The Sun accounts for 99.86% of the mass in our solar system.

questions.text: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
questions.explanation: Venus rotates very slowly, taking 243 Earth days to rotate once on its axis.

questions.text: Glass is actually a slow-moving liquid, which is why old windows are thicker at the bottom.
questions.explanation: Glass is an amorphous solid. Old glass is thicker at the bottom due to early manufacturing techniques.

questions.text: There are more trees on Earth than stars in the Milky Way.
questions.explanation: There are ~3 trillion trees on Earth, compared to ~100-400 billion stars in the Milky Way.

questions.text: Water cannot exist as liquid, solid, and gas simultaneously.
questions.explanation: At the 'triple point', water exists in all three states at once.

questions.text: Carrots dramatically improve your night vision.
questions.explanation: You would need a severe deficiency to see any real benefit from extra carrots.

questions.text: Old glass windows are thicker at the bottom because glass flows slowly.
questions.explanation: Glass is an amorphous solid. Old windows are thicker due to manufacturing techniques.

questions.text: Humans share less than 10% of their DNA with any plant.
questions.explanation: Humans share about 50% of their DNA with bananas due to shared cellular machinery.

questions.text: Cold water boils faster than room temperature water.
questions.explanation: Warmer water needs less energy to reach boiling point and heats faster.

questions.text: Diamonds are made from compressed coal.
questions.explanation: They form from pure carbon under extreme pressure, before coal-producing plants even existed.

questions.text: Helium is the only element that cannot solidify at normal atmospheric pressure.
questions.explanation: Quantum effects prevent it from solidifying without added pressure.

questions.text: The universe is approximately 13.8 billion years old.
questions.explanation: Based on measurements of cosmic background radiation.

questions.text: Hot water can freeze faster than cold water.
questions.explanation: Known as the Mpemba effect, it is still debated by scientists.

questions.text: Peanuts are classified as nuts.
questions.explanation: Peanuts are legumes, biologically related to beans and lentils.

questions.text: An atom is mostly empty space.
questions.explanation: If an atom were a stadium, the nucleus would be the size of a marble.

questions.text: Gold is the best electrical conductor.
questions.explanation: Silver is the best conductor; gold is used for its corrosion resistance.

questions.text: Tomatoes are botanically classified as fruit.
questions.explanation: Any structure enclosing seeds that develops from a flower is botanically a fruit.

questions.text: The human body has far more human cells than bacterial cells.
questions.explanation: There are actually more bacterial cells (~38 trillion) than human cells (~30 trillion).

questions.text: All neon signs use neon gas.
questions.explanation: Only reddish-orange signs use pure neon; other colors use argon, krypton, or other gases.

questions.text: The Sun appears yellow but is actually a white star.
questions.explanation: Earth atmosphere scatters blue light, making the Sun look yellow.

questions.text: Sound travels faster through water than through air.
questions.explanation: Molecules in water are closer together, transmitting vibrations more quickly.

questions.text: Rubber is a good electrical conductor.
questions.explanation: Rubber is an excellent insulator, which is why wires are coated with it.

questions.text: The human eye can distinguish roughly 10 million colors.
questions.explanation: Possible due to three types of cone cells with overlapping sensitivity ranges.

questions.text: There are more trees on Earth than stars in the Milky Way.
questions.explanation: About 3 trillion trees on Earth vs an estimated 100 to 400 billion stars.

questions.text: Wi-Fi stands for Wireless Fidelity.
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi is a trademarked name with no official meaning; it was coined as a play on Hi-Fi.

questions.text: The human stomach gets a new lining every four days.
questions.explanation: Stomach acid is so corrosive that the lining must be continuously replaced.

questions.text: Humans and T-Rex are closer in time than T-Rex and Stegosaurus.
questions.explanation: Stegosaurus lived 80 million years before T-Rex; we are only 65 million years from T-Rex.

questions.text: There are more possible chess games than atoms in the observable universe.
questions.explanation: The Shannon number estimates 10 to the power of 120 possible chess games.

questions.text: Oxygen is the most abundant element in Earth crust.
questions.explanation: It makes up about 46 percent of the crust by mass, mostly locked in silicate minerals.

questions.text: Lightning is hotter than the surface of the sun.
questions.explanation: A lightning bolt reaches 30,000 Kelvin; the sun surface is about 5,778 Kelvin.

questions.text: Humans can smell rain because of a chemical called petrichor.
questions.explanation: Petrichor is produced when rain hits dry earth, releasing oils and geosmin.

questions.text: Fingernails grow faster on your dominant hand.
questions.explanation: Increased blood flow from use stimulates faster nail growth.

questions.text: The appendix has no function in the human body.
questions.explanation: It is believed to serve as a reservoir for gut bacteria and may play an immune role.

questions.text: Saturn would sink in a giant ocean of water.
questions.explanation: Saturn's average density is less than water, so it would actually float.

questions.text: Adults have more bones than babies.
questions.explanation: Babies have about 300 bones; many fuse as we grow, leaving adults with just 206.

questions.text: Dark matter makes up the majority of the universe.
questions.explanation: About 27 percent dark matter, 68 percent dark energy, and only 5 percent normal matter.

questions.text: The smell of cut grass is a distress signal from plants.
questions.explanation: Damaged grass releases green leaf volatiles to warn neighboring plants.

questions.text: Copper door handles self-disinfect.
questions.explanation: Copper has oligodynamic properties that kill microbes within hours.

questions.text: You can physically see the Milky Way galaxy from Earth.
questions.explanation: We are inside it, so we see it as a band of stars across the night sky.

questions.text: There are more stars in the universe than grains of sand on Earth beaches.
questions.explanation: Estimates put observable universe stars at around 10 to the power of 24.

questions.text: Clouds are weightless.
questions.explanation: An average cumulus cloud weighs about 500 tonnes.

questions.text: The human brain is about 95% protein.
questions.explanation: The brain is actually about 75% water.

questions.text: Two pieces of metal will permanently fuse if touched in outer space.
questions.explanation: Called cold welding, it happens because there is no oxide layer to prevent bonding.

questions.text: Gravity travels at the speed of light.
questions.explanation: Gravitational waves were confirmed to travel at exactly the speed of light.

questions.text: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain above sea level.
questions.explanation: It stands at 8,848 meters.

questions.text: The Great Wall of China is visible from space with the naked eye.
questions.explanation: It is a myth; you cannot see it without aid from low Earth orbit.

questions.text: There are 50 states in the United States.
questions.explanation: There are exactly 50 states.

questions.text: Napoleon Bonaparte was extremely short.
questions.explanation: He was around 5'6", which was average height for a Frenchman at the time.

questions.text: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire.
questions.explanation: Oxford was founded around 1096, while the city of Tenochtitlán was founded in 1325.

questions.text: Vikings wore horned helmets in battle.
questions.explanation: There is no historical evidence that Vikings ever wore horned helmets.

questions.text: Toasters were invented before pre-sliced bread.
questions.explanation: The electric toaster was invented in 1893, while sliced bread came out in 1928.

questions.text: Humans use only 10% of their brains.
questions.explanation: We use virtually every part of the brain, and most parts are active almost all the time.

questions.text: Maine is the closest US state to Africa.
questions.explanation: The Quoddy Head peninsula in Maine is closer to Africa than any part of Florida.

questions.text: Cleopatra lived closer to the iPhone's invention than to the building of the Great Pyramid.
questions.explanation: She lived around 30 BC, whereas the Great Pyramid was built around 2560 BC.

questions.text: The Eiffel Tower stays exactly the same height year-round.
questions.explanation: It can grow up to 15 cm taller in summer due to thermal expansion of the iron.

questions.text: Fortune cookies were invented in China.
questions.explanation: They were invented in California in the early 1900s.

questions.text: Brazil has more lakes than any other country.
questions.explanation: Canada has approximately 2 million lakes — more than the rest of the world combined.

questions.text: Every continent has at least one McDonald's.
questions.explanation: Antarctica has no McDonald's or any permanent commercial food chain.

questions.text: Australia is wider than the Moon.
questions.explanation: Australia spans almost 4000 km across; the Moon is only 3474 km in diameter.

questions.text: English is the official national language of the United States.
questions.explanation: The US has no federally designated official language.

questions.text: More people are killed by vending machines than by sharks each year.
questions.explanation: Tipping incidents cause several deaths annually vs roughly 10 shark fatalities worldwide.

questions.text: French fries were invented in France.
questions.explanation: They are believed to have originated in Belgium.

questions.text: The letter Q does not appear in any US state name.
questions.explanation: Check all 50 state names and you will find not a single Q.

questions.text: Alaska is both the westernmost and easternmost US state.
questions.explanation: Its Aleutian Islands cross the 180 degree meridian into the Eastern Hemisphere.

questions.text: Pineapples grow on trees and ripen within a few weeks.
questions.explanation: Pineapples grow on ground-level plants and take about two years to mature.

questions.text: A jiffy is an actual scientific unit of time.
questions.explanation: It equals the time light takes to travel one centimeter, about 33 picoseconds.

questions.text: The unicorn is the national animal of Scotland.
questions.explanation: Adopted in the 12th century as the natural rival of England lion symbol.

questions.text: Humans can fully digest cellulose from plants.
questions.explanation: We lack the cellulase enzyme, so dietary fiber passes through us undigested.

questions.text: The Great Wall of China is visible from space with the naked eye.
questions.explanation: Astronauts have confirmed this is impossible without telescopic aids.

questions.text: Tsar Nicholas II and King George V of England were first cousins.
questions.explanation: They looked so alike that even palace staff sometimes confused them.

questions.text: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the Moon landing than to the construction of the Great Pyramid.
questions.explanation: The pyramids predate her by 2500 years; the Moon landing was only 2000 years after her.

questions.text: Strawberries have their seeds on the inside.
questions.explanation: Strawberries are the only fruit with seeds on the outside — each dot is a tiny achene.

questions.text: Mount Chimborazo is closer to outer space than Mount Everest.
questions.explanation: The equatorial bulge means Chimborazo peak is the farthest point from Earth center.

questions.text: Honey never expires.
questions.explanation: Honey found in Egyptian tombs over 3000 years old was still perfectly edible.

questions.text: The hashtag symbol is called an octothorpe.
questions.explanation: Bell Labs engineers coined the term in the 1960s when adding it to phone keypads.

questions.text: A baker's dozen is 12 because bakers wanted to save money.
questions.explanation: A baker's dozen is actually 13 — medieval bakers added an extra to avoid fines.

questions.text: Nintendo was founded in 1889.
questions.explanation: It started as a playing card company, long before video games existed.

questions.text: The inventor of the World Wide Web received a knighthood.
questions.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee was knighted by Queen Elizabeth II in 2004.

questions.text: Oxford and Cambridge universities have their own railway stations.
questions.explanation: Both cities have their own mainline rail connections.

questions.text: Monaco is the smallest country in the world.
questions.explanation: Vatican City is the smallest at just 0.44 square kilometres.

questions.text: Iceland has no army.
questions.explanation: Iceland has a Coast Guard and a Crisis Response Unit but no traditional standing army.

questions.text: The Sahara Desert is the largest hot desert in the world.
questions.explanation: It covers about 9.2 million square kilometres across North Africa.

questions.text: IKEA is named after its founder.
questions.explanation: Ingvar Kamprad, Elmtaryd (his farm), Agunnaryd (his village).

questions.text: No piece of paper can be folded in half more than eight times.
questions.explanation: Britney Gallivan folded a piece of toilet paper 12 times in 2002, disproving this myth.

questions.text: Sherlock Holmes never actually said Elementary, my dear Watson in the original stories.
questions.explanation: The exact phrase never appears in any of Conan Doyle original works.

questions.text: Cashews grow inside a fruit.
questions.explanation: The cashew apple is a swollen stem; the nut hangs below it in a shell.

questions.text: The symbol on the pound sterling sign is a letter L.
questions.explanation: It stands for libra, the Latin word for balance or scales.

questions.text: Russia has more time zones than any other country.
questions.explanation: Russia spans 11 time zones from Kaliningrad to Kamchatka.

questions.text: The average person walks the equivalent of once around Earth in a lifetime.
questions.explanation: We actually walk about five times around the Earth over a lifetime.

questions.text: Bubble wrap was originally invented as wallpaper.
questions.explanation: Alfred Fielding and Marc Chavannes tried to market it as textured wallpaper in 1957.

questions.text: The shortest commercial flight in the world lasts 90 seconds.
questions.explanation: The Westray to Papa Westray flight in Scotland can take under two minutes.

questions.text: Japan is the most visited country in the world.
questions.explanation: France consistently tops global tourism rankings with about 90 million visitors annually.

questions.text: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.
questions.explanation: One of the more entertaining official collective nouns.

questions.text: The Atlantic Ocean is saltier than the Pacific Ocean.
questions.explanation: The Atlantic has higher salinity due to less rainfall and more evaporation.

questions.text: A standard soccer match lasts for 90 minutes.
questions.explanation: Two halves of 45 minutes.

questions.text: In tennis, the term 'love' means a score of zero.
questions.explanation: It's believed to come from the French word 'l'oeuf' meaning egg (shaped like a zero).

questions.text: Michael Jordan played professional baseball.
questions.explanation: He played Minor League Baseball for the Chicago White Sox organization.

questions.text: The Olympics are held every three years.
questions.explanation: Summer and Winter Olympics are each held every four years.

questions.text: Tug-of-war was once an Olympic sport.
questions.explanation: It was part of the Olympics from 1900 to 1920.

questions.text: Golf balls used to be made out of tightly packed feathers.
questions.explanation: The 'feathery' was a leather pouch stuffed with wet feathers that dried and hardened.

questions.text: A bowling pin needs to only tilt 7.5 degrees to fall over.
questions.explanation: Pins are designed with a very high center of gravity.

questions.text: Babe Ruth holds the record for the most home runs in MLB history.
questions.explanation: Barry Bonds holds the record, followed by Hank Aaron.

questions.text: No boxer in history has ever retired with an undefeated record.
questions.explanation: Rocky Marciano and Floyd Mayweather Jr. both retired undefeated.

questions.text: The longest recorded tennis match lasted over 11 hours.
questions.explanation: John Isner and Nicolas Mahut played for 11 hours and 5 minutes at Wimbledon in 2010.

questions.text: Basketball was originally played with wooden buckets.
questions.explanation: James Naismith used peach baskets, not wooden buckets.

questions.text: A marathon is exactly 26 miles long.
questions.explanation: The official distance is 26 miles and 385 yards.

questions.text: The Tour de France was created to sell newspapers.
questions.explanation: It was launched as a publicity stunt by the sports newspaper L Auto in 1903.

questions.text: The Olympic torch has never accidentally gone out.
questions.explanation: It has been extinguished several times during relay events and discreetly relit.

questions.text: Table tennis was once called whiff-whaff.
questions.explanation: It started as an after-dinner parlor game in Victorian England.

questions.text: Muhammad Ali was born with the name Michael Williams.
questions.explanation: He was born Cassius Marcellus Clay Jr. and changed his name in 1964.

questions.text: Wimbledon requires all players to wear all-white clothing.
questions.explanation: The All England Club strictly enforces this tradition to this day.

questions.text: Hockey pucks are frozen before NHL games.
questions.explanation: Frozen pucks slide faster and bounce less unpredictably on the ice.

questions.text: The longest boxing match in history lasted over 100 rounds.
questions.explanation: Bowen vs Burke in 1893 went 110 rounds and lasted over 7 hours.

questions.text: An NFL game contains only about 11 minutes of actual playing time.
questions.explanation: The rest is replays, huddles, clock stoppages, and commercials.

questions.text: Michael Phelps won fewer gold medals than most Olympic countries.
questions.explanation: His 23 gold medals exceed the all-time totals of the majority of participating nations.

questions.text: Golf was once banned in Scotland.
questions.explanation: King James II banned it in 1457 because men were skipping archery practice.

questions.text: Volleyball was invented as a less strenuous alternative to basketball.
questions.explanation: William G. Morgan created it in 1895 for older businessmen.

questions.text: Players have used the Stanley Cup as a dog bowl.
questions.explanation: Players get personal days with the cup and many have let pets eat from it.

questions.text: Baseball umpires are required to wear black underwear.
questions.explanation: This is so nothing bright shows if their pants split during a game.

questions.text: Sumo wrestlers must weigh over 150 kg to compete professionally.
questions.explanation: There is no minimum weight rule in sumo; technique is what matters.

questions.text: Tennis was originally played with the bare palm of the hand.
questions.explanation: Early jeu de paume, French for palm game, was played with open hands.

questions.text: Olympic gold medals are made of solid gold.
questions.explanation: Modern gold medals are mostly silver with only a gold plating.

questions.text: Usain Bolt top recorded speed was over 35 mph.
questions.explanation: His top recorded speed was 27.78 mph, still extraordinarily fast.

questions.text: Competitive eating is an official Olympic sport.
questions.explanation: It has never been part of the Olympic program.

questions.text: The first modern Olympic Games were held in Rome in 1900.
questions.explanation: The first modern Olympics were held in Athens in 1896.

questions.text: In the original Olympic Games, athletes competed in the nude.
questions.explanation: Greek athletes competed naked as a celebration of the human form.

questions.text: A cricket ball is heavier than a tennis ball.
questions.explanation: Cricket balls weigh 155-163g; tennis balls are only 56-59g.

questions.text: Cristiano Ronaldo is the highest paid athlete in history.
questions.explanation: Michael Jordan's total earnings adjusted for inflation exceed most active athletes.

questions.text: The longest ever tennis rally lasted over 600 shots.
questions.explanation: A practice rally of 643 shots was recorded in 2012.

questions.text: Chess is officially recognized as a sport by the International Olympic Committee.
questions.explanation: It was recognized in 1999, though it has never been an Olympic event.

questions.text: Serena Williams won a Grand Slam title while pregnant.
questions.explanation: She won the 2017 Australian Open in the early weeks of her pregnancy.

questions.text: The New York Yankees have the most World Series titles.
questions.explanation: They have won 27 World Series championships.

questions.text: In soccer, a hat trick refers to scoring five goals.
questions.explanation: A hat trick means scoring three goals in a single match.

questions.text: The fastest recorded tennis serve exceeded 250 km per hour.
questions.explanation: Sam Groth holds the record at 263.4 km per hour.

questions.text: NBA hoops are exactly 12 feet off the ground.
questions.explanation: They are exactly 10 feet (3.05 meters) — unchanged since 1891.

questions.text: Polo is the oldest team sport still played today.
questions.explanation: It originated in ancient Persia around 2500 years ago.

questions.text: Tiger Woods turned professional golfer at age 20.
questions.explanation: He turned professional at age 20 in 1996.

questions.text: The Super Bowl has been played on Sundays every year.
questions.explanation: Super Bowl LX in 2026 was moved to a Saturday, breaking the tradition for the first time.

questions.text: An ice hockey puck travels faster than 160 km per hour during play.
questions.explanation: The hardest slap shots have been recorded above 190 km per hour.

questions.text: Germany has won the FIFA World Cup more times than any other country.
questions.explanation: Brazil holds the record with five FIFA World Cup titles.

questions.text: In baseball, three strikes means you are out.
questions.explanation: A fundamental rule of the sport since its codification in 1845.

questions.text: Jockeys in horse racing must weigh under 60 kg.
questions.explanation: Weight limits vary by race, but many races allow up to around 63 kg including equipment.

questions.text: The Olympic rings represent the five continents of the world.
questions.explanation: Africa, Americas, Asia, Europe, and Oceania, united by the interlinked rings.

questions.text: Badminton shuttlecocks can travel faster than 300 km per hour.
questions.explanation: The world record smash speed is 493 km per hour, making it the fastest projectile in sport.

questions.text: The Titanic sank on its maiden voyage.
questions.explanation: It sank in 1912 while traveling from Southampton to New York.

questions.text: George Washington had wooden teeth.
questions.explanation: His dentures were made from ivory, lead, brass, and other humans' teeth.

questions.text: Julius Caesar was the first Emperor of Rome.
questions.explanation: Augustus was the first Roman Emperor.

questions.text: The shortest war in history lasted only 38 minutes.
questions.explanation: The Anglo-Zanzibar War of 1896.

questions.text: Abraham Lincoln is in the Wrestling Hall of Fame.
questions.explanation: He was an outstanding wrestler in his youth, losing only one out of 300 matches.

questions.text: Paul Revere shouted 'The British are coming!'
questions.explanation: The mission relied on secrecy, so he quietly warned local leaders.

questions.text: The ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash.
questions.explanation: Ammonia in urine was considered a bleaching agent.

questions.text: Witches were burned at the stake during the Salem Witch Trials.
questions.explanation: Most were hanged, and one was pressed to death. None were burned.

questions.text: There was a famous Pope who dug up his predecessor's corpse to put it on trial.
questions.explanation: The 'Cadaver Synod' of 897, where Pope Formosus was posthumously tried.

questions.text: Albert Einstein was offered the presidency of Israel.
questions.explanation: He was offered the role in 1952 but politely declined.

questions.text: Cleopatra was ethnically Egyptian.
questions.explanation: She was of Greek Macedonian descent from the Ptolemaic dynasty.

questions.text: The Hundred Years War lasted exactly 100 years.
questions.explanation: It lasted 116 years, from 1337 to 1453.

questions.text: The Egyptian pyramids were built by slaves.
questions.explanation: Archaeological evidence shows they were built by organized paid workers.

questions.text: Abraham Lincoln held a US patent.
questions.explanation: He patented a device to lift boats over shallow water, the only president to do so.

questions.text: Alexander the Great never lost a battle.
questions.explanation: He fought for 15 years across three continents without a single military defeat.

questions.text: Roman gladiators rarely fought to the death.
questions.explanation: They were expensive to train; promoters preferred surrenders over fatalities.

questions.text: Oxford University existed before the Aztec Empire.
questions.explanation: Oxford dates to around 1096; Tenochtitlan was founded in 1325.

questions.text: Marie Antoinette said Let them eat cake.
questions.explanation: No credible historical evidence exists that she ever said this.

questions.text: Winston Churchill was born in a ladies cloakroom.
questions.explanation: His mother went into premature labor at a ball at Blenheim Palace.

questions.text: More people lived in the Americas than in Europe before Columbus arrived.
questions.explanation: Estimates place the pre-Columbian Americas population at 60 to 100 million.

questions.text: Magellan was the first person to sail around the entire world.
questions.explanation: He died in the Philippines; his crew under Elcano actually completed the voyage.

questions.text: Thomas Edison personally invented the light bulb.
questions.explanation: He improved and commercialized it; Joseph Swan had earlier working prototypes.

questions.text: In Ancient Egypt, killing a cat was punishable by death.
questions.explanation: Cats were considered sacred, and harming one was a serious crime.

questions.text: The shortest recorded war in history lasted only 38 minutes.
questions.explanation: The Anglo-Zanzibar War of 1896 lasted between 38 and 45 minutes.

questions.text: Joan of Arc was burned at the stake for witchcraft.
questions.explanation: She was convicted of heresy and gender nonconformity for wearing men clothing.

questions.text: Ancient Rome had a goddess dedicated to the city sewer system.
questions.explanation: Cloacina was the deity protecting the Cloaca Maxima, Rome main sewer.

questions.text: Napoleon Bonaparte was unusually short for his era.
questions.explanation: He was around 5 foot 7, average for a French man of his time.

questions.text: Albert Einstein failed maths at school.
questions.explanation: He excelled at maths and physics; the myth comes from a misread of Swiss report cards.

questions.text: Albert Einstein was offered the presidency of Israel and declined.
questions.explanation: He was offered the role in 1952 and politely turned it down.

questions.text: The Viking helmet with horns is a historical myth.
questions.explanation: No horned Viking helmets have ever been found by archaeologists; it was invented by artists.

questions.text: Ancient Greeks and Romans had a concept of human rights.
questions.explanation: Modern human rights are a post-Enlightenment concept; ancient societies had no equivalent.

questions.text: The first photograph ever taken required an eight-hour exposure.
questions.explanation: Joseph Nicephore Niepce took the earliest surviving photo around 1826-27.

questions.text: The Great Fire of London destroyed St Paul Cathedral.
questions.explanation: The old Gothic cathedral burned down; Wren designed its famous replacement.

questions.text: Julius Caesar was assassinated on the Ides of March, or March 15th.
questions.explanation: He was stabbed 23 times by a group of senators in 44 BC.

questions.text: The Berlin Wall fell in 1991.
questions.explanation: The Berlin Wall fell on 9 November 1989; Germany reunified in October 1990.

questions.text: Ancient Egyptians shaved their eyebrows when a pet cat died.
questions.explanation: It was a sign of mourning; they also shaved eyebrows when dogs died.

questions.text: The Colosseum in Rome could hold up to 80,000 spectators.
questions.explanation: Modern estimates put its capacity between 50,000 and 80,000 people.

questions.text: King Henry VIII had six wives and executed all of them.
questions.explanation: He had six wives but only executed two; others were divorced or died naturally.

questions.text: Gandhi was nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize multiple times but never won.
questions.explanation: He was nominated five times; the Nobel Committee later expressed regret at not awarding him.

questions.text: The Titanic sank on her maiden voyage in 1912.
questions.explanation: She sank on 15 April 1912 after hitting an iceberg.

questions.text: Genghis Khan killed so many people that global temperatures dropped.
questions.explanation: His campaigns depopulated vast areas, allowing forests to regrow and absorb enough CO2 to affect climate.

questions.text: The first country to give women the right to vote was the United States.
questions.explanation: New Zealand was the first in 1893; the US followed in 1920.

questions.text: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be demolished after 20 years.
questions.explanation: It was built as a temporary structure for the 1889 World Fair.

questions.text: Michelangelo was still working as an architect in his 80s.
questions.explanation: He worked on St Peter Basilica in Rome until close to his death at age 88.

questions.text: The Roman Empire and the Han Dynasty of China coexisted at the same time.
questions.explanation: Both were superpowers from roughly 200 BC to 200 AD and even knew of each other.

questions.text: Ancient Chinese emperors were buried with armies of terracotta soldiers.
questions.explanation: China First Emperor Qin Shi Huang had over 8,000 life-size soldiers buried with him.

questions.text: The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was larger than most European cities at the time of conquest.
questions.explanation: With 200,000 to 300,000 people, it dwarfed contemporary London or Paris.

questions.text: Christopher Columbus discovered America.
questions.explanation: Indigenous peoples lived there for millennia; Norse explorer Leif Erikson also preceded Columbus.

questions.text: Isaac Newton discovered gravity when an apple fell on his head.
questions.explanation: The apple story is largely mythologized; he observed falling apples but was not struck by one.

questions.text: Socrates never wrote any books or texts himself.
questions.explanation: All we know of Socrates comes through the writings of his students like Plato.

questions.text: The FIFA World Cup is contested by national teams, not clubs.
questions.explanation: Fact: The men's FIFA World Cup is played by national teams representing their countries.

questions.text: The first FIFA World Cup was held in Uruguay.
questions.explanation: Fact: Uruguay hosted the first FIFA World Cup in 1930 and also won it.

questions.text: The World Cup final is played over two legs.
questions.explanation: Fiction: The World Cup final is a single match.

questions.text: A World Cup group-stage match can end in a draw.
questions.explanation: Fact: Group-stage matches can finish level, with each team receiving one point.

questions.text: A team must win every match to win the World Cup.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Teams can draw or even lose earlier matches and still win the tournament.

questions.text: The World Cup has always been played every four years without interruption.
questions.explanation: Fiction: The 1942 and 1946 tournaments were cancelled because of World War II.

questions.text: Brazil has won more men's World Cups than any other country.
questions.explanation: Fact: Brazil have won the men's FIFA World Cup five times.

questions.text: Only European teams have ever won the men's FIFA World Cup.
questions.explanation: Fiction: South American teams such as Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay have won it.

questions.text: The host nation automatically qualifies for the World Cup.
questions.explanation: Fact: The host nation receives automatic qualification for the tournament.

questions.text: Argentina won the 2022 FIFA World Cup.
questions.explanation: Fact: Argentina beat France in the 2022 final in Qatar.

questions.text: The 2022 World Cup was played during the usual June-July window.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Qatar 2022 was played in November and December.

questions.text: Penalty shoot-outs can decide World Cup knockout matches.
questions.explanation: Fact: Knockout matches tied after extra time can be decided by penalties.

questions.text: The winner keeps the original World Cup trophy forever.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Winners receive the trophy temporarily and are given a replica to keep.

questions.text: South Africa hosted the first World Cup on African soil.
questions.explanation: Fact: South Africa 2010 was the first men's FIFA World Cup hosted in Africa.

questions.text: Qatar hosted the first World Cup held in the Middle East.
questions.explanation: Fact: Qatar 2022 was the first men's FIFA World Cup held in the Middle East.

questions.text: The 2026 World Cup will have 48 teams.
questions.explanation: Fact: The 2026 men's World Cup expands from 32 to 48 teams.

questions.text: The 2026 World Cup will be hosted by two countries.
questions.explanation: Fiction: It will be hosted by Canada, Mexico, and the United States.

questions.text: Brazil has missed at least one men's World Cup.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Brazil have appeared in every men's FIFA World Cup through 2022.

questions.text: Morocco was the first African team to reach a men's World Cup semi-final.
questions.explanation: Fact: Morocco reached the semi-finals at Qatar 2022.

questions.text: A red card means the player can be replaced immediately.
questions.explanation: Fiction: A sent-off player cannot be replaced during the match.

questions.text: The World Cup includes a group stage before knockouts.
questions.explanation: Fact: Modern World Cups use a group phase followed by knockout rounds.

questions.text: England has won the men's World Cup more than once.
questions.explanation: Fiction: England's only men's World Cup title came in 1966.

questions.text: Pele won three men's World Cups as a player.
questions.explanation: Fact: Pele was part of Brazil's winning squads in 1958, 1962, and 1970.

questions.text: The first World Cup final was played between Uruguay and Argentina.
questions.explanation: Fact: Uruguay beat Argentina 4-2 in the 1930 final.

questions.text: The World Cup final has always avoided penalty shoot-outs.
questions.explanation: Fiction: The 1994 final was decided by a penalty shoot-out.

questions.text: The 1950 World Cup did not have a traditional one-match final.
questions.explanation: Fact: The champion was decided through a final round-robin group.

questions.text: The first World Cup penalty shoot-out happened before the 1980s.
questions.explanation: Fiction: The first World Cup penalty shoot-out was West Germany vs France in 1982.

questions.text: The 1998 World Cup was the first men's edition with 32 teams.
questions.explanation: Fact: France 1998 expanded the tournament to 32 teams.

questions.text: The 1970 World Cup introduced yellow and red cards.
questions.explanation: Fact: The yellow and red card system was introduced at Mexico 1970.

questions.text: The first physical red card at a World Cup was shown in 1970.
questions.explanation: Fiction: The card system began in 1970, but the first physical red card came in 1974.

questions.text: The World Cup trophy was once found by a dog.
questions.explanation: Fact: The stolen Jules Rimet Trophy was found in 1966 by a dog named Pickles.

questions.text: The original Jules Rimet Trophy is still displayed at FIFA headquarters.
questions.explanation: Fiction: It was stolen in Brazil in 1983 and has never been recovered.

questions.text: The current FIFA World Cup Trophy replaced the Jules Rimet Trophy after 1970.
questions.explanation: Fact: The current trophy was introduced after Brazil kept the Jules Rimet Trophy permanently.

questions.text: The Jules Rimet Trophy was stolen twice.
questions.explanation: Fact: It was stolen in England in 1966 and again in Brazil in 1983.

questions.text: The original Jules Rimet Trophy was recovered after its 1983 theft.
questions.explanation: Fiction: It was stolen in Brazil in 1983 and has never been recovered.

questions.text: The black-and-white Telstar ball was designed partly for television visibility.
questions.explanation: Fact: Its design helped the ball stand out on black-and-white television.

questions.text: The 1970 World Cup used the first adidas World Cup match ball.
questions.explanation: Fact: The adidas Telstar debuted at Mexico 1970.

questions.text: Miroslav Klose is the top scorer in men's World Cup history.
questions.explanation: Fact: Klose scored 16 World Cup goals for Germany.

questions.text: Ronaldo Nazario scored more World Cup goals than Miroslav Klose.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Ronaldo scored 15; Klose holds the men's record with 16.

questions.text: Just Fontaine scored all his World Cup goals in one tournament.
questions.explanation: Fact: Fontaine scored 13 goals at the 1958 World Cup.

questions.text: No player has scored more than ten goals in a single men's World Cup.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Just Fontaine scored 13 goals in 1958.

questions.text: Pele scored in a World Cup final before turning 18.
questions.explanation: Fact: Pele scored in the 1958 final as a 17-year-old.

questions.text: A player has scored five goals in one men's World Cup match.
questions.explanation: Fact: Oleg Salenko scored five for Russia against Cameroon in 1994.

questions.text: The most goals by one player in a World Cup match is four.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Oleg Salenko scored five in one match in 1994.

questions.text: Roger Milla scored at a men's World Cup after turning 40.
questions.explanation: Fact: Milla scored for Cameroon at age 42 in 1994.

questions.text: Lionel Messi has more men's World Cup appearances than any other player.
questions.explanation: Fact: Messi reached 26 men's World Cup appearances.

questions.text: The World Cup Golden Boot always goes to a player from the winning team.
questions.explanation: Fiction: The top scorer often comes from another team.

questions.text: France won their first men's World Cup before Brazil won their first.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Brazil first won in 1958; France first won in 1998.

questions.text: A World Cup final has ended goalless after extra time.
questions.explanation: Fact: Brazil vs Italy in 1994 finished 0-0 after extra time.

questions.text: Germany has played in a men's World Cup final decided by penalties.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Germany's men's World Cup finals have not been decided by penalty shoot-outs.

questions.text: The 1930 World Cup had no qualification tournament.
questions.explanation: Fact: The first World Cup was invitation-based rather than qualification-based.

questions.text: All 1930 World Cup matches were played in one city.
questions.explanation: Fact: All matches at the 1930 World Cup were played in Montevideo.

questions.text: The 1954 World Cup had a match with twelve total goals.
questions.explanation: Fact: Austria beat Switzerland 7-5 in 1954.

questions.text: Austria beat Switzerland 7-4 in the highest-scoring World Cup match.
questions.explanation: Fiction: The match finished 7-5, with twelve total goals.

questions.text: The fastest World Cup goal came after less than twenty seconds.
questions.explanation: Fact: Hakan Sukur scored after about 11 seconds in 2002.

questions.text: The fastest World Cup goal was scored in a final.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Hakan Sukur scored it in the 2002 third-place match.

questions.text: A World Cup final hat-trick has happened more than once.
questions.explanation: Fact: Geoff Hurst did it in 1966 and Kylian Mbappe did it in 2022.

questions.text: Kylian Mbappe was the first player to score a World Cup final hat-trick.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Geoff Hurst scored a World Cup final hat-trick in 1966.

questions.text: Spain won the 2010 World Cup after losing their opening match.
questions.explanation: Fact: Spain lost to Switzerland in their opener and still won the tournament.

questions.text: Spain conceded a goal in the 2010 World Cup knockout stage.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Spain kept clean sheets in all four knockout matches in 2010.

questions.text: Italy once won the World Cup as host nation.
questions.explanation: Fact: Italy hosted and won the 1934 World Cup.

questions.text: England won the World Cup before ever hosting it.
questions.explanation: Fiction: England won their only title as hosts in 1966.

questions.text: A goalkeeper has been sent off in a World Cup match.
questions.explanation: Fact: Italy's Gianluca Pagliuca was sent off at the 1994 World Cup.

questions.text: The first physical World Cup red card was shown to a goalkeeper.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Carlos Caszely of Chile received the first physical World Cup red card in 1974.

questions.text: The first World Cup sending-off happened before red cards existed.
questions.explanation: Fact: Players were sent off before the physical red-card system was introduced.

questions.text: Carlos Caszely received the first physical red card in World Cup history.
questions.explanation: Fact: Caszely was sent off for Chile against West Germany in 1974.

questions.text: The 1994 World Cup final was played indoors.
questions.explanation: Fiction: It was played outdoors at the Rose Bowl in Pasadena.

questions.text: India has played at the men's FIFA World Cup.
questions.explanation: Fiction: India qualified by default for 1950 but withdrew and has never played in the tournament.

questions.text: Brazil has won a men's World Cup hosted in Europe.
questions.explanation: Fact: Brazil won the 1958 World Cup in Sweden.

questions.text: No South American team has won a men's World Cup hosted in Europe.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Brazil won the 1958 World Cup in Sweden.

questions.text: Brazil only needed a draw in their final 1950 match to become champions.
questions.explanation: Fact: Brazil would have won the 1950 title by avoiding defeat against Uruguay.

questions.text: Uruguay needed only a draw against Brazil in 1950 to become champions.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Uruguay had to beat Brazil in the decisive 1950 match, which they did.

questions.text: The World Cup has been won by a host nation more than once.
questions.explanation: Fact: Host winners include Uruguay, Italy, England, Argentina, and France.

questions.text: Every men's World Cup host has reached at least the semi-finals.
questions.explanation: Fiction: Several hosts have failed to reach the semi-finals.

questions.text: Mexico has hosted the men's FIFA World Cup more than once.
questions.explanation: Fact: Mexico hosted the tournament in 1970 and 1986, and is a co-host again in 2026.

questions.text: Scotland were eliminated from the 1974 World Cup without losing a match.
questions.explanation: Fact: Scotland beat Zaire and drew with Brazil and Yugoslavia, but went out on goal difference.

questions.text: The 1974 World Cup was the first to use the current trophy design.
questions.explanation: Fact: The current FIFA World Cup Trophy debuted in 1974 after Brazil kept the Jules Rimet Trophy.

questions.text: A team once won the World Cup after scoring only eight goals in the tournament.
questions.explanation: Fact: Spain won the 2010 World Cup while scoring only eight goals in seven matches.

questions.text: Spain won every knockout match at the 2010 World Cup by the same scoreline.
questions.explanation: Fact: Spain won all four knockout matches in 2010 by 1-0.

questions.text: The first World Cup final used the same ball for both halves.
questions.explanation: Fiction: The 1930 final used Argentina's ball in the first half and Uruguay's ball in the second.

questions.text: A defending champion has been eliminated from the next World Cup group stage.
questions.explanation: Fact: Several defending champions have gone out in the group stage, including France in 2002, Italy in 2010, Spain in 2014, and Germany in 2018.
